summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers should have a deep appreciation for computer hardware, not just software. He recommends three books that celebrate computing history through photography: 'Core Memory' (a visual tour of the Computer History Museum), 'The Computer: An Illustrated History' (a broad pictorial history from abacus to iPod), and 'The Encyclopedia of Game Machines' (an exhaustive reference of gaming hardware). Atwood shares his experience visiting the Computer History Museum and playing Spacewar on the only functioning PDP-1 in existence. The post is a love letter to the physical machines that make programming possible.
key_points: Programmers who love their craft should also appreciate the hardware that enables it
The Computer History Museum in Mountain View is an essential destination for anyone interested in computing
Three recommended books provide different angles on hardware history: museum pieces, cultural context, and gaming systems
Physical computing artifacts, when preserved and documented through photography, become objects of beauty and historical significance
Hardware appreciation is a legitimate and rewarding aspect of programmer culture
money_quotes: I've never understood programmers who loved the craft of programming, but were disinterested in the underlying hardware – the very tool that allows them to practice their craft.
I have an unabashed love for computer hardware that borders on inappropriate. I'm not ashamed to admit it.
That's how I had the privilege of playing Spacewar on the original vector display of the only known functioning PDP-1 in the world.
Boing Boing called them magnificent portraits of the machines' pretty faces and equally beautiful guts, a stunning series of 'glamour shots' for nerds.
It's the only way to satisfy my insatiable hardware lust, short of the world's largest bank account.
key_insight: The physical machines that enable programming are worthy of the same passion and appreciation that programmers give to their code.
